Can we get more info regarding the daily limit on the playground? Is it just counting how many were run within the last 24 hours, giving me back one run as they free up? Or is it starting and stopping counting at some specific time window?

@_**Rhys Edwards|145** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/9-general/topic//near/799):
```quote
Can we get more info regarding the daily limit on the playground? Is it just counting how many were run within the last 24 hours, giving me back one run as they free up? Or is it starting and stopping counting at some specific time window?
```

based on my data, It's a rolling 24-hour window, not a fixed daily reset. Each task occupies a slot for exactly 24 hours from the moment it was created. As each task ages past 24h, that slot frees up individually.

 So if you submitted 10 tasks between 8:00 PM and 11:00 PM yesterday, you'd get slots back one-by-one between 8:00 PM and 11:00 PM today — not all at once at midnight.

 You can verify this by noting the timestamps of your submissions and checking when new submissions start working again. The error message says "10 tasks/day" but it's actually a rolling window, not a calendar day.

 That's derived directly from my data — submitted 10 tasks between 8:52 PM and 12:00 AM ET, then the next day could only submit 4 more at 9:51 PM because exactly 4 of yesterday's tasks had aged past 24h by then. The remaining 6 freed up between 11:37 PM and midnight, perfectly matching their creation timestamps + 24 hours.

Hi! Would it be possible to expose model sampling parameters (temperature, top_p, max_tokens) in the playground or submission form? Different approaches may benefit from different settings — for example, deterministic outputs (temperature=0) vs. exploratory reasoning. This would also help reproduce results more accurately across different testing environments. Thanks!

Hi! There seems to be a bug with credit calculation. The UI shows 4 credits required for a run, but actual cost per run is ~0.7 credits. This forces users to manually remove problems to fit within the displayed limit, even though actual spend would be within budget. Could this be fixed so the displayed estimate matches actual cost? Thanks!

@_**Garbid|157** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/9-general/topic//near/918):
```quote
Hi! There seems to be a bug with credit calculation. The UI shows 4 credits required for a run, but actual cost per run is ~0.7 credits. This forces users to manually remove problems to fit within the displayed limit, even though actual spend would be within budget. Could this be fixed so the displayed estimate matches actual cost? Thanks!
```
Not a bug — it's a **credit hold (reservation) system**. The `creditsCost` displayed upfront is reserved from your balance immediately, but the actual charge (`creditsActual`) settles much lower after the task completes. The difference is released back.

I've tracked this across 24 paid tasks. Here's what the hold-to-actual ratios look like per model:

| Model | Hold/problem | Actual/problem | Ratio (hold/actual) |
|-------------------|--------------|----------------|---------------------|
| gpt-oss-120b | ~0.40–1.00 | ~0.10 | 4–10x |
| Llama 3.3 70B | ~0.75–1.00 | ~0.075 | 10x |
| Grok 4.1 Fast | ~1.00 | ~0.15 | 6–10x |
| Gemini Flash Lite | ~2.67 | ~0.18 | 11–20x |

What I've discovered:

- **Holds are the real bottleneck, not actual cost.** With 10.0 daily credits, you can afford ~100 gpt-oss problems by actual cost, but holds will block you much sooner.
- **Holds release when the task completes.** Submitting sequentially (wait for task to finish before submitting the next) lets you recycle the freed hold budget.
- **Batching helps.** Packing multiple problems into one task reduces the per-problem hold. E.g., 20 problems × gpt-oss holds 7.0 credits (0.35/problem) vs 1 problem holding 1.0 (1.0/problem). Actual cost is the same either way (~0.10/problem).
- **Gemini has the steepest hold** — a single 3-problem Gemini task holds 8.0 credits, which is most of your daily budget, even though actual cost is only ~0.70.

The display showing 4 credits isn't what you'll be charged — it's what's reserved while the task runs. Once it finishes, you'll see only ~0.7 deducted and the rest returned.

Are you able to use up all your credits each day? I have a surplus of 8.80 credits each day that I was never able to use as I keep approaching and reaching this 10/10 daily task limiter that stops me from any more tests.

Adapting the cheatsheets to saturate each new benchmark is genuinely impressive - it demonstrates how effectively models can capture the statistical structure of a given "training" set. But this is essentially classic overfitting to one specific slice of the magma distribution.

For true generalization, we can go one level deeper: distilling the actual underlying laws that govern the entire magma space itself, and then translating those principles into clear, LLM-friendly language within a single prompt. (This representation will be slightly different for each model - DeepSeek, for example, tends to be more literal than the others and benefits from logical steps in pseudocode.)

This challenge truly lives up to its name - it really is fundamentally a distillation problem.

On the public datasets, current approaches seem to be memorising "how it looks", which works well until the distribution shifts. In contrast, successfully distilling the real generative principles reveals "why it must be so". Once you have those principles, the same representation can generalise beautifully to unseen problems without requiring constant remakes.

Techniques such as symbolic regression or sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy) excel at extracting compact, human-interpretable equations that extrapolate far beyond the training data. It also helps to think in terms of elaborative encoding - linking new implications to a coherent network of first principles rather than simply memorising the structure of a random collection of isolated examples.

I'm excited about this challenge because it aligns perfectly with my work over the past 15 months on "law discovery and distillation" in mathematical physics. If we collectively shift toward real law-distillation (instead of standard ML tricks), I think we'll break the cycle of remakes and unlock far more robust, generalisable performance across the whole magma space. Looking forward to seeing what the community discovers next!

43 messages were moved here from #**general>** by @_**Terence Tao|28**.

My offline testing has been revealing some interesting patterns across the full 4694-law space, so I thought I'd share a few in case they help others.


* Mining laws directly from the implication graph looks genuinely promising. Not just hand-written heuristics, but recurring structural implication patterns extracted from the full law space, not only the public subsets.
 
* Strong **FALSE** laws seem much easier to mine than strong **TRUE** laws. The FALSE signals I'm seeing are generally more stable and higher precision.
 
* **New variable pressure** looks super important. If EQ2 appears to require genuinely new structure or variables that EQ1 does not force, that is often a strong FALSE cue.
 
* **Shape loss / compression** matters a lot. Node count, distinct-variable count, subtree relations, and overlap keep showing up as useful structural signals.
 
* A conservative **FALSE-first** routing strategy may work better than broad TRUE rescue. Wide TRUE rescue rules tended to overfire in my testing.
 
* TRUE rules seem noisier and weaker overall. The more robust path may be a small number of conservative TRUE triggers plus stronger FALSE eliminators.
 
* Held-out validation alone does not seem sufficient. Full-matrix checking on finalist rules is extremely valuable, since some rules that look good on samples become much less trustworthy globally.
 
* Prompt style matters a lot by model. Some models respond better to strict procedural logic than to natural-language explanation.
 
* Binary-only output control is harder than it looks. Models often still try to explain unless the formatting constraints are very tight. Pseudocode-style prompting seems to help.
 
* There seems to be real value in separating **checker behavior** from **mathematician behavior**. For this task, reliable procedural checking often beats fluent freeform reasoning.
 
My current takeaway is that Stage 1 may be less about finding one magical proof prompt, and more about mining robust structural FALSE laws, then adding only a very small number of conservative TRUE laws on top, and finally translating that into prompt form as faithfully as possible.
TLDR; mine strong FALSE laws first, small number of conservative TRUE triggers, distrust broad TRUE rescue, validate finalists globally, and turn the result into a strict procedural checker prompt.

Hi I'd like to share a suggestion, for the final eval pipeline of stage 1, to better stick to a fixed model hosted by a fixed provider, instead of letting OpenRouter route the requests, or at least use a fixed inference provider via OpenRouter. Because based on my experience and experiments done, different providers' different inference framework set up and sometimes quantizations sometimes does heavily affect the behaviour of the models.

Here is an example:
image.png

the 3rd column is done via OpenRouter with BYOK and fixed to route to Groq. You can see that the parse rate does changes alot.

I also inspected the failure mode at response level, this behaviour of only replying 'VER' in the end(my prompt instruct model to output VERDICT: TRUE or FALSE in the end) also is only associated with some provider (not Groq), while not hitting the max_token wall at all:
image.png

Please consider, thanks!

messages.content:
After a few days of testing I'm torn on the 8k token limit.

I'm seeing a significant performance gap between 8k and 16k on gpt-oss-120b and gemma-4-31b. At 8k, a meaningful fraction of calls silently fail — the models burn through their budget on internal reasoning before producing any visible output, so you get an empty final channel and an unparseable response.

The practical effect is that the challenge at 8k feels less like "teach small models to solve equational implication problems" and more like "prevent reasoning models from over-thinking themselves out of an answer." At 16k, the problem shifts back toward the actual math.

If the hosts are open to revisiting this detail, it would meaningfully change where I focus my effort over the next few days. Curious whether others are running into the same thing.

Thanks
messages.subject: general chat

messages.content: Thanks for the feedback. We will optimize the evaluation setup based on the feedback from the community.

messages.content:
Some of my thoughts on the objective of this competition and the eval pipeline:

The objective is clearly not only about the knowledge compression, that task was already done in the ML effort part of the ETP project.

So if the competition focuses more on finding an efficient and effective black-box prompt optimization technique on math reasoning task that generalize across data. Then there is not much value in having a weighted metric across models. In practice, if this generalizable (across data) technique exists, no one would use the same optimized prompt across models. We would always optimize a specific prompt for each model.

An analogy would be: There are mathematicians all over the world that speaks different languages: English, Chinese, Spanish, Russian... It's less likely that there exists a universal language that we can communicate with all these mathematicians and expect them to understand equally well. It makes better sense to communicate with each of them in their native tongue.

So what I'd suggest is:
We can stick with one model, gpt-oss-120b at low reasoning at 0 temp and maximum max_completion_tokens = 64k (half of it's full context length). Give it enough budge to complete reasoning + procedure following, at low reasoning effort, it would rarely reach near this limit but just in case.

This way, it also saves the community's cost on running our search algorithm by 2/3.

Once the prompt optimization method is proven to be effective and generalizable for this one model, it can be easily adapted for other models as well, just plug and play.

Anyway just a thought to consider. @YZ

messages.content:
Gavin Litchfield said:

r a few days of testing I'm torn on the 8k token limit.

I'm seeing a significant performance gap between 8k and 16k on gpt-oss-120b and gemma-4-31b. At 8k, a meaningful fraction of calls silently fail — the models burn through their budget on internal reasoning before producing any visible output, so you get an empty final channel and an unparseable response.

The practical effect is that the challenge at 8k feels less like "teach small models to solve equational implication problems" and more like "prevent reasoning models from over-thinking themselves out of an answer." At 16k, the problem shifts back toward the actual math.

If the hosts are open to revisiting this detail, it would meaningfully change where I focus my effort over the next few days. Curious whether others are running into the same thing.

@Gavin Litchfield Thanks for sharing the insights. We discussed this internally, and we understand the concern that 8192 can be tight for reasoning-heavy models in some cases. Our current plan is to keep 8192 as the primary Stage 1 setting and main leaderboard, while also running an additional 16384-token evaluation and showing those results in a separate leaderboard.

messages.content:
YZ said:

Hi Heath Sanchez ! The leaderboard for the contributor network on the hard2 problem set (200 problems) has been added. Thanks!

Thanks so much, @YZ - really appreciate how quickly you added hard2.

This is already a big step forward.

I still think an average across all public sets would be the most useful overall view, since it would better reflect whether a cheatsheet is generalizing rather than just doing well on one slice. A single-set leaderboard is definitely helpful, but averaging across the public sets would make it much easier to spot the strongest all-round cheatsheets.

At the moment, I could publish five different cheatsheets, each tuned to a different public set, and each could look extremely strong in isolation while transferring poorly to unseen problems (which is how they will ultimately be evaluated). That also makes it harder for others to judge which shared cheatsheets are actually useful for their submissions.

Held-out performance would be the ideal measure of course, but average public-set performance feels like the best practical proxy to nudge cheatsheets in the right direction.

messages.content:
Heath Sanchez said:

Adapting the cheatsheets to saturate each new benchmark is genuinely impressive - it demonstrates how effectively models can capture the statistical structure of a given "training" set. But this is essentially classic overfitting to one specific slice of the magma distribution.

For true generalization, we can go one level deeper: distilling the actual underlying laws that govern the entire magma space itself, and then translating those principles into clear, LLM-friendly language within a single prompt. (This representation will be slightly different for each model - DeepSeek, for example, tends to be more literal than the others and benefits from logical steps in pseudocode.)

This challenge truly lives up to its name - it really is fundamentally a distillation problem.

On the public datasets, current approaches seem to be memorising "how it looks", which works well until the distribution shifts. In contrast, successfully distilling the real generative principles reveals "why it must be so". Once you have those principles, the same representation can generalise beautifully to unseen problems without requiring constant remakes.

Techniques such as symbolic regression or sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy) excel at extracting compact, human-interpretable equations that extrapolate far beyond the training data. It also helps to think in terms of elaborative encoding - linking new implications to a coherent network of first principles rather than simply memorising the structure of a random collection of isolated examples.

I’m excited about this challenge because it aligns perfectly with my work over the past 15 months on "law discovery and distillation" in mathematical physics. If we collectively shift toward real law-distillation (instead of standard ML tricks), I think we'll break the cycle of remakes and unlock far more robust, generalisable performance across the whole magma space. Looking forward to seeing what the community discovers next!

This resonates. We run a Lean 4 discovery engine (10,500+ verified theorems from x²=x+1) and the pattern is identical — the AI discovers structural invariants (spine classifications, fiber multiplicities, spectral gaps) much faster than it constructs proofs. The discovery phase is pure routing: "this structure has property X, route to separation lemma Y." The verification phase is traditional proof.

What's interesting for this challenge: the structural FALSE laws you're mining from the implication graph are exactly the kind of thing our system produces — combinatorial obstructions that can be checked mechanically. Adam McKenna's Spine Isolation Theorem (posted in the Discovery thread here) is a clean example. We contributed a follow-up on his repo exploring whether ring-operation magmas from Fibonacci modular arithmetic give additional separating power beyond cyclic successor magmas.

The router-vs-mathematician split might be the key architectural insight for Stage 2 — route to the right proof strategy first, then execute. Curious whether your FALSE-law mining has surfaced any divisibility-based patterns (our Zeckendorf/Fibonacci structures generate a lot of those).

Repo if useful: https://github.com/the-omega-institute/automath

Happy to return the favor for anyone who needs a review or formalization check.

Hi Wenlin — really appreciate the thoughtful note.

Your framing around structural invariant discovery resonates directly with what I document in the paper — the router hypothesis: models aren't constructing proofs step-by-step, they're routing into latent structural templates. What you're describing from the formal side (discovery phase = structural routing, verification phase = traditional proof) is the same phenomenon from a different angle.

The FALSE-law mining angle is especially interesting. Many failure modes I observed are consistent with misrouting to near-miss structures rather than a lack of reasoning capacity per se — which aligns with what your system surfaces as structural separators.

Would love to compare notes on how your system represents and selects invariants vs. how prompts implicitly induce them. Happy to connect at hello@bytepro.ai.

messages.content: @YZ I’ve noticed a rather major issue where LLM intelligence seems to fluctuate based on the time of day. Using the exact same cheatsheet and model, I got completely different results at different hours. For example, when I tested Gemma 31B at 8 AM GMT, it scored 9/10, but by 9 PM GMT, it dropped to 5/10 and the cheatsheet just didn't work anymore. This is quite worrying because it suggests LLM reliability might depend on current user traffic rather than the logic of our cheatsheet. I'm concerned this could really skew the results we've submitted and how the competition is being evaluated.
messages.subject: general chat

messages.content:
BlackNull.com said:

YZ I’ve noticed a rather major issue where LLM intelligence seems to fluctuate based on the time of day. Using the exact same cheatsheet and model, I got completely different results at different hours. For example, when I tested Gemma 31B at 8 AM GMT, it scored 9/10, but by 9 PM GMT, it dropped to 5/10 and the cheatsheet just didn't work anymore. This is quite worrying because it suggests LLM reliability might depend on current user traffic rather than the logic of our cheatsheet. I'm concerned this could really skew the results we've submitted and how the competition is being evaluated.

Yes, this can be super annoying. I do not think this is just traffic, but a more general feature of how LLMs behave.

With the same model and the same cheatsheet or prompt, you can still get different outcomes because the model is not a deterministic symbolic engine in practice. This is actually a major limitation of current LLMs that often goes unnoticed, because it is less obvious than things like hallucinations. Small differences in inference trajectory, backend routing, retries, or runtime conditions can push the model into a different reasoning path, and that can materially change accuracy on a small test set. That is one reason current LLM architecture is still not reliable enough for science-level use.

It makes the planned 3 repeats per model/problem pair very important for the evaluation. Without repeated runs, a cheatsheet score may mostly reflect one particular reasoning trajectory rather than its true average performance.
messages.subject: general chat

messages.content: The cheatsheet performs optimally between 4:00 and 8:00 AM (GMT 0), when the LLM provider, DeepIntra, experiences lower traffic congestion. I recommend that SAIR run cheatsheet independently to ensure maximum accuracy. Through repeated testing during this specific window, I have confirmed that the cheatsheet remains highly stable. The inconsistencies I've observed are clearly traffic-related, which significantly impacts the evaluation process. When over 1,000 questions and multiple cheatsheets are processed simultaneously, the resulting traffic spike causes greater deviations in the results than the LLM’s own reasoning errors.

messages.content:
Hi everyone,

I wanted to share a project that might be of interest to this community. I've been analyzing the discussions from this Zulip channel and put together an independent deep research report on the SAIR Math Distillation Challenge.
GitHub Repository: https://github.com/Feishu-China/SAIR-Insights
Interactive Bilingual Preview: https://feishu-china.github.io/SAIR-Insights/

The report is available in both Chinese and English, and covers the full lifecycle of Stage 1 — from the competition background and timeline to core technical discoveries, participant ecosystem, and open questions. It includes dedicated sections on:

  • Prompt ordering matters: The community discovery that placing cheatsheets before task descriptions significantly improves results, both through API caching and better model performance
  • Terence Tao's AlphaEvolve predictors and the difficulty of building truly diverse hard sets
  • Het S's creative 3D visualization of the Magma problem space using fingerprinting + clustering
  • The evolution of submission formats and community infrastructure discussions
  • Model hallucination and self-contradiction: Models finding counterexamples yet constructing fake proofs to overturn their own findings
  • Manuel Cázares' systematic study of 45+ prompt variants and the "Single-Prompt Ceiling" phenomenon — including the striking finding that the best local result (79.25%) dropped to 55.5% on the official benchmark
  • Cross-distribution generalization challenges: Only 1 of 52 submissions exceeded 65% on both hard2 and hard3 simultaneously
  • The Spine Isolation Theorem (McKenna et al.): Zero exceptions across 1.54M implication pairs

All analysis is based on publicly available discussions from this channel (414 messages). The full reports (Chinese & English markdown) and an interactive bilingual HTML page are available in the repo.

Feedback, corrections, and contributions are very welcome — especially if I've mischaracterized anyone's work or missed important discussions. The repo has a CONTRIBUTING.md for anyone who'd like to contribute.

Hope this is useful for the community!

messages.content:
@YZ I've been building some Stage 2 infra around MathGraph: a verification kernel that tries to send each implication to one of three terminal forms: Lean proof, finite countermodel, or named obstruction, then distills accepted results into reusable proof routes, countermodel families, and a queryable lawbook.

I'm planning to make the queryable API open source, because the pattern seems useful far beyond ETP. I'm still focused on submitting a strong Stage 2 solver, but I'd generally like to align my longer-term work with what would actually help SAIR / AI-for-science.

If you had to rank these by most long-term value, what would you put first?

  1. maximizing accepted certificate volume
  2. discovering reusable Lean lemmas / proof motifs
  3. generating compact countermodel families
  4. building a general interpretable obstruction atlas
  5. something else entirely

My guess is that "named obstruction + reusable constructor/lemma" is more scientifically useful than raw certificate volume, but I’d love to know if that’s the right north star beyond the comp.

Any insight appreciated!
messages.subject: general chat

messages.content:
@Heath Sanchez This is a strong direction. I think you are asking exactly the right question: not only how to solve more Stage 2 instances, but how to turn those solutions into reusable mathematical infrastructure.

My view is that raw accepted certificate volume is important, but mostly as evidence and fuel. The longer-term value comes when certificates can be compressed into reusable structure: named obstructions, countermodel constructors, Lean lemmas, proof routes, and searchable patterns that transfer to new problems.

That is very close to what we hope SAIR Competitions can become. The competition gives the community a shared task, judge, datasets, and tools; the deeper goal is to provide an open framework that the community can extend, improve, and use to push the frontier forward.

Stage 2 has been open for less than 10 days, and the community has already produced very strong results. Excited to see how far everyone can push this.
messages.subject: general chat

messages.content:
YZ said:

Heath Sanchez This is a strong direction. I think you are asking exactly the right question: not only how to solve more Stage 2 instances, but how to turn those solutions into reusable mathematical infrastructure.

My view is that raw accepted certificate volume is important, but mostly as evidence and fuel. The longer-term value comes when certificates can be compressed into reusable structure: named obstructions, countermodel constructors, Lean lemmas, proof routes, and searchable patterns that transfer to new problems.

That is very close to what we hope SAIR Competitions can become. The competition gives the community a shared task, judge, datasets, and tools; the deeper goal is to provide an open framework that the community can extend, improve, and use to push the frontier forward.

Stage 2 has been open for less than 10 days, and the community has already produced very strong results. Excited to see how far everyone can push this.

Thanks @YZ, that’s really encouraging. The distinction between certificate volume as fuel and reusable structure as the real output is exactly the line I was trying to clarify.

One thing I'm testing now is whether MathGraph can make that compression explicit: not just "this pair is solved", but "this pair factors through this proof route / countermodel constructor / obstruction family."

The strongest signal so far is a residual-style one: after closing under the source law, either the target appears absorbed, suggesting a proof/Lean route, or a residual model space survives, suggesting a countermodel/obstruction route. I'm being careful not to overclaim it as a theorem yet, but it feels like the right object to distill, especially because the same pattern is starting to transfer beyond ETP magma implications into Boolean CNF, finite graph property, and rewrite/string-system implication worlds.

So I'll keep pushing toward named obstruction + reusable constructor/lemma as the north star, with accepted certificates as the evidence base. Thanks again
